---
title: Forecasting in Python
bibliography: references.bib
author:
  - name: Amy Heather
    orcid: 0000-0002-6596-3479
---

```{=html}
<script src="finisher-header.es5.min.js" type="text/javascript"></script>

<body>
  <div class="header finisher-header" style="width: 2000px; height: 900px;">
    Logo, navigation, etc.
  </div>
  ...
</body>

<script type="text/javascript">
new FinisherHeader({
  "count": 12,
  "size": {
    "min": 328,
    "max": 862,
    "pulse": 0
  },
  "speed": {
    "x": {
      "min": 0.3,
      "max": 0.5
    },
    "y": {
      "min": 0.1,
      "max": 0.3
    }
  },
  "colors": {
    "background": "#660033",
    "particles": [
      "#a86b7e",
      "#c69ba7",
      "#691c1c"
    ]
  },
  "blending": "overlay",
  "opacity": {
    "center": 1,
    "edge": 0.1
  },
  "skew": 0,
  "shapes": [
    "c"
  ]
});
</script>
```

A beginner-friendly introduction to time-series forecasting.

These are my own notes, written to consolidate my understanding as I learned this topic. They draw heavily on a handful of excellent existing resources. The key sources I used are:

- @monks_intro_to_forecasting — a general introduction to forecasting concepts and workflow.
- @monks_arima — a focused walkthrough of ARIMA modelling.
- @hyndman_toolbox — the standard toolbox reference for forecasting methods and best practice.
- @suleman_metrics — a guide to forecast evaluation metrics.

### References

::: {#refs}
:::
